summary:
The fall of the Hittite Empire around 1178–1180 BC was one of the defining events of the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]], ending more than four centuries of Hittite imperial power in Anatolia and the Near East. The destruction was sudden and total: [[event:1818|Hattusa]], the Hittite capital, was burned to the ground, its population dispersed, and the Hittite state never reconstituted itself. The causes are debated but most likely involved a combination of external invasion by the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]], internal political instability, and severe drought and famine.

The last Hittite king, Suppiluliuma II, left inscriptions describing naval battles off Cyprus and military campaigns, but the empire's final collapse occurred within years of these records. Letters found at [[event:1833|Ugarit]] — itself destroyed at the same time — describe a Hittite king writing desperately for grain shipments, suggesting famine was a pressing crisis. The destruction layer at Hattusa shows evidence of fire but also of an orderly removal of valuable objects, suggesting the population had some warning and was not simply massacred.

After the fall of Hattusa, Hittite culture survived in Neo-Hittite city-states in southeastern Anatolia and northern Syria, including Carchemish, Melid, and Tabal. These states preserved Hittite language, script, and artistic traditions for several more centuries before being absorbed by the expanding Assyrian Empire in the ninth and eighth centuries BC. They also served as important conduits transmitting Hittite artistic motifs and religious ideas into the broader Near Eastern cultural sphere.
